items.content:
Client wrote on 12/01/2026 at 2:30 PM:
My partner and I separated three weeks ago after five years together. We have two children, a daughter aged 7 and a son aged 4. I moved out and I am currently staying with the children at my mother's apartment. It is very cramped — the children share a small room and my son has been wetting the bed again since the move. My ex-partner wants the children every weekend, but my daughter refuses to go. She says she is angry at her father. I work part-time as a nurse and my shifts change weekly, so a fixed visitation schedule is difficult. I have no idea how to handle the legal side of custody.

Counsellor wrote on 13/01/2026 at 9:15 AM:
Thank you for reaching out. It sounds like you are dealing with a lot of changes at once — a new living situation, your children's emotional reactions, and the question of custody arrangements. Bed-wetting in young children after a major life change is very common and usually temporary. Regarding your daughter's refusal, children often express loyalty conflicts through anger. Have you been able to talk with your ex-partner about a flexible schedule that accounts for your shift work? For the legal aspects, I would recommend contacting your local family counselling centre — they offer free mediation for separating parents.

Client wrote on 15/01/2026 at 8:45 PM:
Thank you for your response. The situation has gotten worse this week. My ex showed up unannounced at my mother's place on Wednesday and there was an argument in front of the children. My daughter locked herself in the bathroom and would not come out for an hour. My mother says I should get a lawyer immediately, but I cannot afford one right now. My son has started asking every morning whether Daddy is coming today. I feel completely overwhelmed and I am not sleeping well.
items.subject: Custody concerns after separation

items.content:
Client wrote on 05/01/2026 at 6:12 PM:
I am writing because my 14-year-old son has refused to go to school for the past three weeks. Every morning he complains of headaches and stomach aches, but our paediatrician found nothing physically wrong. Before this started, he was an average student and had a small group of friends. My son will not tell me what happened. His form teacher called and said he has 15 unexcused absences now. I am a single father and I have already used all my remaining leave days from work to stay home with him. I am worried about his grades and whether the school will involve the youth welfare office.

Counsellor wrote on 06/01/2026 at 10:00 AM:
Thank you for writing. School refusal at this age often signals that something significant has changed in the social environment. The physical symptoms your son reports are real — they are a stress response, even without an organic cause. It may help to gently explore whether there has been conflict with peers. Sometimes children at this age experience social exclusion or bullying but feel ashamed to talk about it. I would suggest requesting a meeting with the school counsellor, ideally without your son present at first, to understand what the school has observed. The youth welfare office is usually involved only in prolonged cases and their role is supportive, not punitive.

Client wrote on 08/01/2026 at 9:30 PM:
I spoke with his form teacher again today. She mentioned that a group of boys in his class has been making comments about his clothes and his phone being old. She was not sure if it counts as bullying. When I carefully asked my son about it tonight, he started crying and said everyone hates him and he never wants to go back. He also said he has been eating lunch alone for two months. I feel terrible that I did not notice earlier.
items.subject: School refusal and possible bullying

items.content:
Client wrote on 08/01/2026 at 10:22 PM:
My 16-year-old daughter has been coming home after midnight almost every weekend for the past month. Last Saturday she came home at 2 AM smelling of alcohol. When I confronted her, she screamed at me that I am controlling and that all her friends have more freedom. She slammed her door so hard that the frame cracked. My husband thinks we should take her phone away, but I am afraid that will make things worse. She used to tell me everything, and now she barely speaks to us. Her grades have dropped from mostly Bs to Ds this term. She also quit the volleyball team last month without telling us.

Counsellor wrote on 09/01/2026 at 11:00 AM:
Thank you for reaching out. What you are describing is very typical of the tensions that arise during mid-adolescence — the push for autonomy often comes in waves that feel sudden and extreme to parents. The alcohol use and the late nights are worth taking seriously, but the way you address them matters more than the specific consequence you choose. Taking the phone might create a power struggle, while a clear conversation about safety expectations could open a door. Can you identify any specific trigger — did something change in her social group or at school around the time this started?

Client wrote on 11/01/2026 at 7:15 PM:
I tried to have a calm conversation with her last night, as you suggested. It went poorly at first, but then she admitted that her best friend since primary school moved away in October and she has been hanging out with a new group. She said they think curfews are embarrassing and she does not want to be the only one who has to leave early. She also said she feels like we do not trust her. I am not sure how to set boundaries without pushing her further away.
items.subject: Conflicts with teenage daughter about boundaries

items.content:
Client wrote on 10/01/2026 at 11:45 PM:
I am a single mother of three children — a 10-year-old daughter, an 8-year-old son, and a 3-year-old son. I work full-time as an administrative assistant. My ex-husband moved to another city two years ago and pays child support irregularly. Most evenings I do not sit down before 10 PM. I have had recurring headaches for months and my doctor says it is tension-related. Last week I shouted at my 3-year-old for spilling juice and I frightened him so badly he would not come near me for the rest of the evening. I felt horrible. My oldest daughter has started taking on tasks like making sandwiches for her brothers and I can see that it is too much responsibility for her age. I have no family nearby and I cannot afford a babysitter.

Counsellor wrote on 12/01/2026 at 8:30 AM:
Thank you for sharing this with me. What you are describing sounds like an enormous workload and I want you to know that reaching out is an important step. The fact that you noticed your reaction to your youngest and reflected on it shows real self-awareness. Parental burnout is a recognised condition and it does not mean you are a bad mother. Your oldest taking on caregiving tasks is a common pattern in stressed families, and it is good that you are aware of it. Have you looked into local family support services? Many communities offer subsidised after-school care or family helpers. I would also recommend checking whether your employer offers an employee assistance programme.

Client wrote on 14/01/2026 at 10:10 PM:
I called the family support centre you mentioned. They have a six-week waiting list. In the meantime, my 8-year-old has started acting out at school — his teacher says he is pushing other children and refusing to sit still. I had to leave work early twice this week for calls from the school. My boss was understanding the first time but made a comment today about reliability. I feel like everything is falling apart and I do not know which problem to tackle first.
items.subject: Exhaustion and burnout as a single parent

items.content:
Client wrote on 03/01/2026 at 4:30 PM:
I moved in with my new partner six months ago. He has a 9-year-old daughter from his previous marriage who lives with us every other week. I have an 11-year-old son from my first marriage who lives with us full-time. The children do not get along. My son says he hates sharing his room when she is here and calls her a guest who should go home. My partner's daughter told her mother that I am mean to her, which is not true — I simply asked her to help clear the table. Now my partner's ex-wife is threatening to go back to court. My partner feels caught in the middle and we have been arguing almost every night. Last weekend my son asked if he could go live with his father instead.

Counsellor wrote on 05/01/2026 at 9:45 AM:
Thank you for writing. Blended families are one of the most complex family structures, and the adjustment period often takes much longer than parents expect — research suggests 2 to 5 years for a blended family to find its rhythm. Your son's reaction to sharing his room is about territory and belonging, not about your partner's daughter personally. And the daughter's complaint to her mother is very common — children in shared custody often test boundaries by playing households against each other. The most important thing right now is that you and your partner present a united front. Would you consider family counselling together? Many of these dynamics respond well to guided conversations.

Client wrote on 07/01/2026 at 8:20 PM:
Things escalated on Saturday. During dinner my son made a comment about the food and my partner corrected him. My son said you are not my dad and ran to his room. My partner was hurt and said maybe the children are just not ready for this. Later that night my partner's daughter called her mother crying and now the ex-wife has sent a formal letter through her lawyer. I feel like I am losing my partner and my son at the same time.
items.subject: Blended family adjustment difficulties

items.content:
Client wrote on 06/01/2026 at 3:15 PM:
I have been diagnosed with moderate depression six months ago and I am on medication. I have a 10-year-old son who I raise together with my wife. Since the diagnosis, I struggle to get through the day. Most mornings I cannot get out of bed before my wife has already taken our son to school. On weekends I often stay in bed until noon. My son asked my wife last week why Daddy is always sad and whether it is his fault. That broke my heart. My wife has been incredibly patient, but I can see she is exhausted from carrying everything alone. I used to coach my son's football team, but I had to step down two months ago because I could not face the other parents. My therapist says I should try to maintain routines, but even small tasks feel overwhelming.

Counsellor wrote on 07/01/2026 at 10:30 AM:
Thank you for reaching out. Living with depression while parenting is enormously challenging, and the guilt you feel about its impact on your son is something many parents in your situation share. Your son's question about whether it is his fault is developmentally very normal — children naturally centre themselves in family dynamics. It might help to have an age-appropriate conversation with him about how sometimes grown-ups feel unwell in their thoughts and feelings, just like people can feel unwell in their body. This takes the mystery away without burdening him with details. Is your wife receiving any support herself? Partners of people with depression often develop compassion fatigue.

Client wrote on 09/01/2026 at 9:45 PM:
I tried talking to my son this weekend, loosely following what you suggested. He asked if I was going to get better and I told him honestly that I am working on it. He hugged me and said he would help. My wife and I also talked — she admitted she has been feeling resentful and exhausted. She said she needs me to at least handle the morning routine twice a week so she can go to work without rushing. I want to do that, but I am not sure I can manage it reliably. My medication was adjusted last week and the side effects make mornings even harder.
items.subject: Dealing with depression as a parent

items.content:
Client wrote on 07/01/2026 at 7:00 PM:
My 12-year-old son spends five to six hours a day on his computer, mostly playing an online multiplayer game. On weekends it is even more — sometimes eight or nine hours. His grades have dropped significantly this term. He went from being in the top third of his class to nearly failing mathematics and English. He has also stopped playing football, which he used to love, and rarely sees his friends in person anymore. When I try to set time limits he becomes extremely angry and once threw his controller at the wall. He says all his friends play even more than he does and that I do not understand because I grew up without the internet. My husband thinks it is just a phase, but I think it is getting out of control.

Counsellor wrote on 08/01/2026 at 9:00 AM:
Thank you for reaching out. The pattern you describe — increasing play time, withdrawal from previous activities, declining academic performance, and aggressive reactions to limits — goes beyond a typical hobby. At 12, the social component of online gaming is very powerful because the friendships feel real and immediate. A sudden restriction can feel like social exclusion to him. Rather than removing the computer entirely, I would suggest starting with a structured plan: agree on specific times together, tie gaming time to completed homework, and offer one offline social activity as an alternative. Have you spoken with his teachers about what they observe in class?

Client wrote on 10/01/2026 at 8:30 PM:
I spoke with his maths teacher today. She said he has been falling asleep in class and has not submitted homework in three weeks. She also said he seems withdrawn and does not interact with classmates during breaks. At home, I tried to talk to him calmly about a screen time plan. He agreed to one hour on weekdays if he can play freely on Saturday. But by Tuesday he had already broken the agreement and when I turned off the router he screamed for twenty minutes and punched a hole in his bedroom door. My husband finally admitted this is serious. We do not know if we need a therapist or a technology intervention or both.
items.subject: Excessive screen time and declining grades

items.content:
Client wrote on 09/01/2026 at 5:30 PM:
We moved to Germany from Syria four years ago. My husband works in a warehouse and I am currently in a language course, B1 level. We have three children — a 15-year-old daughter, a 12-year-old son, and a 6-year-old daughter. My oldest daughter speaks fluent German and translates for us at doctor appointments and parent-teacher meetings. I can see this burden is unfair to her. My son is struggling at school because his German is not strong enough for the Gymnasium he attends — his teacher suggested he move to Realschule, which feels like a failure. My 6-year-old speaks mostly German and sometimes does not understand when we speak Arabic at home. I worry we are losing our culture. My husband thinks the children should focus on German only, but I want them to keep Arabic. We also feel isolated — we have friendly neighbours but no close friends in the community.

Counsellor wrote on 10/01/2026 at 10:15 AM:
Thank you for sharing your situation. The challenges you describe are very common in families navigating integration, and they often create tension between practical needs and cultural identity. Your daughter's role as interpreter is called language brokering — research shows it can be both empowering and stressful for children. It would be helpful to reduce her translating responsibilities where possible, for example by using professional interpreters for medical visits. Regarding your son, a move to Realschule is not a failure — it is a different pathway and many students transfer back later. Bilingualism is a strength, not a conflict. Would your family be open to connecting with a local integration counselling service? They often organise family activities that help with both language and community.

Client wrote on 12/01/2026 at 8:00 PM:
My daughter came home crying on Thursday. A classmate told her she would never really be German no matter how well she speaks. She said she does not feel Syrian anymore either and does not know where she belongs. My son refused to go to school on Friday because he is embarrassed about his accent. And my youngest told me she does not want to speak Arabic because the other children in kindergarten said it sounds funny. I feel like I am failing all three of my children in different ways.
items.subject: Cultural integration and identity struggles

items.content:
Client wrote on 11/01/2026 at 12:30 PM:
My 17-year-old son was offered an apprenticeship as an electrician in a city 200 kilometres away starting in August. He wants to accept it and move into a shared flat with another apprentice. My husband and I think he is too young. He has never lived alone, he cannot cook, and he barely manages to keep his room clean. He also has mild asthma that requires daily medication, and I am worried about who will make sure he takes it. When we told him we would prefer him to find something closer, he accused us of holding him back and not believing in him. He has been cold and distant for a week now. His older sister moved out at 21 and she thinks we are being overprotective. My husband is more open to it than I am, which makes me feel like the only one saying no.

Counsellor wrote on 12/01/2026 at 11:00 AM:
Thank you for writing. Your concerns are completely understandable — letting a 17-year-old move 200 kilometres away touches on deep parental instincts. At the same time, your son receiving an apprenticeship offer is a significant achievement and his enthusiasm suggests real motivation. Many young people at 17 are ready for more independence than parents expect. The practical concerns — cooking, medication, daily routines — are solvable with preparation. Rather than a yes or no, would you consider a structured transition? For example, visiting the city together, meeting the flatmate, setting up a medication reminder system, and agreeing on regular check-ins. This shows trust while providing a safety net.

Client wrote on 14/01/2026 at 6:45 PM:
We tried your suggestion of a visit. We drove there last weekend and saw the flat — it was small but clean. The other apprentice seemed responsible. My son was different during the trip, more grown up somehow. He showed us where the company is and had already researched the nearest doctor. On the drive home my husband said he thinks we should let him go. I am still afraid, but I also realized that if we say no, he might resent us. He has agreed to a daily video call for the first month and to come home every second weekend. I suppose my question now is how to prepare him and myself for this.
items.subject: Teenager wanting to move out for apprenticeship

items.content:
Client wrote on 04/01/2026 at 3:00 PM:
I have two children, a daughter aged 11 and a son aged 8. My daughter is academically gifted — she reads constantly, gets excellent grades, and teachers praise her regularly. My son is more physical and creative but struggles with reading and writing. He was recently tested and shows signs of dyslexia, though the formal assessment is still pending. The problem is that my son has started saying things like you love her more and she gets everything. Last week he destroyed a drawing his sister made for a school project. My daughter then said she wished she was an only child. My husband and I try to be fair, but we spend more time helping our daughter with her advanced projects and homework while our son mostly plays outside. I realize now that our son might interpret this as favouritism. My own parents compared me to my sister constantly, and I swore I would never do that.

Counsellor wrote on 05/01/2026 at 10:45 AM:
Thank you for sharing this. The dynamic you describe is very common in families where children have different strengths and needs. Your son is not wrong in his perception — children are very sensitive to how parental time and attention are distributed, even when parents intend to be fair. The key distinction is between equal treatment and equitable treatment. Equal means giving the same to both; equitable means giving each child what they need. Right now, your daughter's academic achievements receive visible recognition, while your son's strengths in physical activity and creativity may feel invisible to him. I would suggest creating dedicated one-on-one time with each child — even 20 minutes of focused attention daily makes a measurable difference. How does your son express his creativity?

Client wrote on 07/01/2026 at 7:30 PM:
Your question about creativity made me think. My son actually builds the most incredible things with Lego and cardboard — last month he built a working marble run that took him three days. But we never displayed it the way we hang up his sister's art and certificates. I tried dedicated time this week. I spent 30 minutes with my son building something together on Tuesday, and he was so happy he told his friend about it. But my daughter noticed and asked why I was spending so much time with him. She said it is not fair because she has to do her homework alone. I feel like whatever I do, one of them feels hurt.
items.subject: Sibling rivalry and unequal treatment concerns

You are an assistant for professionals in psychosocial online counselling. You support counsellors in systematically capturing the current life situation of help-seeking clients. You analyze email threads between clients and counsellors and extract factual information about the client's circumstances.

Analyze the email thread and produce a structured situation description of the client's current life circumstances as a numbered list of up to 3 points.

Each point must contain:
- A concise description of one aspect of the life situation (max 2 sentences)
- Direct quotes from the email thread as evidence, placed after a dash

Focus on: housing situation, family relationships, children (age, development, health), professional/educational situation, health status, social conflicts.

Rules:
- Use only facts from the thread, no speculation
- Avoid introductory phrases, present information directly
- When counselling parents, also include information about their children

Few-shot example:

Input: A single mother reports that her 8-year-old son has been refusing to attend school for two weeks, complaining of stomach aches. The pediatrician found no physical cause. She works part-time and cannot stay home every day.

Output:
1. Single mother working part-time, unable to provide daily supervision due to work obligations. — "I work part-time and cannot stay home every day"
2. 8-year-old son refusing school attendance for two weeks with psychosomatic symptoms. — "my son has been refusing to go to school for two weeks", "he complains of stomach aches but the pediatrician found nothing"

Subject: Custody concerns after separation

Email thread:
Client wrote on 12/01/2026 at 2:30 PM:
My partner and I separated three weeks ago after five years together. We have two children, a daughter aged 7 and a son aged 4. I moved out and I am currently staying with the children at my mother's apartment. It is very cramped — the children share a small room and my son has been wetting the bed again since the move. My ex-partner wants the children every weekend, but my daughter refuses to go. She says she is angry at her father. I work part-time as a nurse and my shifts change weekly, so a fixed visitation schedule is difficult. I have no idea how to handle the legal side of custody.

Counsellor wrote on 13/01/2026 at 9:15 AM:
Thank you for reaching out. It sounds like you are dealing with a lot of changes at once — a new living situation, your children's emotional reactions, and the question of custody arrangements. Bed-wetting in young children after a major life change is very common and usually temporary. Regarding your daughter's refusal, children often express loyalty conflicts through anger. Have you been able to talk with your ex-partner about a flexible schedule that accounts for your shift work? For the legal aspects, I would recommend contacting your local family counselling centre — they offer free mediation for separating parents.

Client wrote on 15/01/2026 at 8:45 PM:
Thank you for your response. The situation has gotten worse this week. My ex showed up unannounced at my mother's place on Wednesday and there was an argument in front of the children. My daughter locked herself in the bathroom and would not come out for an hour. My mother says I should get a lawyer immediately, but I cannot afford one right now. My son has started asking every morning whether Daddy is coming today. I feel completely overwhelmed and I am not sleeping well.

Return only the numbered list. No additional explanations.

outputs.content_preview: The client, a part-time nurse, recently separated from their partner and is currently living with their two children at their mother's apartment. They are facing challenges with their children's re...
outputs.generated_content: The client, a part-time nurse, recently separated from their partner and is currently living with their two children at their mother's apartment. They are facing challenges with their children's reactions to the separation, including bed-wetting and refusal to visit the ex-partner, and are seeking guidance on custody arrangements and the recent conflict that occurred when the ex-partner showed up unannounced.

prompt_variables.content:
Client wrote on 05/01/2026 at 6:12 PM:
I am writing because my 14-year-old son has refused to go to school for the past three weeks. Every morning he complains of headaches and stomach aches, but our paediatrician found nothing physically wrong. Before this started, he was an average student and had a small group of friends. My son will not tell me what happened. His form teacher called and said he has 15 unexcused absences now. I am a single father and I have already used all my remaining leave days from work to stay home with him. I am worried about his grades and whether the school will involve the youth welfare office.

Counsellor wrote on 06/01/2026 at 10:00 AM:
Thank you for writing. School refusal at this age often signals that something significant has changed in the social environment. The physical symptoms your son reports are real — they are a stress response, even without an organic cause. It may help to gently explore whether there has been conflict with peers. Sometimes children at this age experience social exclusion or bullying but feel ashamed to talk about it. I would suggest requesting a meeting with the school counsellor, ideally without your son present at first, to understand what the school has observed. The youth welfare office is usually involved only in prolonged cases and their role is supportive, not punitive.

Client wrote on 08/01/2026 at 9:30 PM:
I spoke with his form teacher again today. She mentioned that a group of boys in his class has been making comments about his clothes and his phone being old. She was not sure if it counts as bullying. When I carefully asked my son about it tonight, he started crying and said everyone hates him and he never wants to go back. He also said he has been eating lunch alone for two months. I feel terrible that I did not notice earlier.

I moved in with my new partner six months ago. He has a 9-year-old daughter from his previous marriage who lives with us every other week. My son says he hates sharing his room when she is here and calls her a guest who should go home. Counsellor wrote on 05/01/2026 at 9:45 AM:
Thank you for writing. Blended families are one of the most complex family structures, and the adjustment period often takes much longer than parents expect — research suggests 2 to 5 years for a blended family to find its rhythm. Your son's reaction to sharing his room is about territory and belonging, not about your partner's daughter personally. And the daughter's complaint to her mother is very common — children in shared custody often test boundaries by playing households against each other. The most important thing right now is that you and your partner present a united front. Would you consider family counselling together? Many of these dynamics respond well to guided conversations.

My son says he hates sharing his room when she is here and calls her a guest who should go home. My partner's daughter told her mother that I am mean to her, which is not true — I simply asked her to help clear the table. Now my partner's ex-wife is threatening to go back to court. My partner feels caught in the middle and we have been arguing almost every night. Last weekend my son asked if he could go live with his father instead.

Counsellor wrote on 05/01/2026 at 9:45 AM:
Thank you for writing. Blended families are one of the most complex family structures, and the adjustment period often takes much longer than parents expect — research suggests 2 to 5 years for a blended family to find its rhythm. Your son's reaction to sharing his room is about territory and belonging, not about your partner's daughter personally. And the daughter's complaint to her mother is very common — children in shared custody often test boundaries by playing households against each other. The most important thing right now is that you and your partner present a united front. Would you consider family counselling together? Many of these dynamics respond well to guided conversations.

Client wrote on 07/01/2026 at 8:20 PM:
Things escalated on Saturday. During dinner my son made a comment about the food and my partner corrected him. My son said you are not my dad and ran to his room. My partner was hurt and said maybe the children are just not ready for this. Later that night my partner's daughter called her mother crying and now the ex-wife has sent a formal letter through her lawyer. I feel like I am losing my partner and my son at the same time.

prompt_variables.content:
Client wrote on 11/01/2026 at 12:30 PM:
My 17-year-old son was offered an apprenticeship as an electrician in a city 200 kilometres away starting in August. He wants to accept it and move into a shared flat with another apprentice. My husband and I think he is too young. He has never lived alone, he cannot cook, and he barely manages to keep his room clean. He also has mild asthma that requires daily medication, and I am worried about who will make sure he takes it. When we told him we would prefer him to find something closer, he accused us of holding him back and not believing in him. He has been cold and distant for a week now. His older sister moved out at 21 and she thinks we are being overprotective. My husband is more open to it than I am, which makes me feel like the only one saying no.

Counsellor wrote on 12/01/2026 at 11:00 AM:
Thank you for writing. Your concerns are completely understandable — letting a 17-year-old move 200 kilometres away touches on deep parental instincts. At the same time, your son receiving an apprenticeship offer is a significant achievement and his enthusiasm suggests real motivation. Many young people at 17 are ready for more independence than parents expect. The practical concerns — cooking, medication, daily routines — are solvable with preparation. Rather than a yes or no, would you consider a structured transition? For example, visiting the city together, meeting the flatmate, setting up a medication reminder system, and agreeing on regular check-ins. This shows trust while providing a safety net.

Client wrote on 14/01/2026 at 6:45 PM:
We tried your suggestion of a visit. We drove there last weekend and saw the flat — it was small but clean. The other apprentice seemed responsible. My son was different during the trip, more grown up somehow. He showed us where the company is and had already researched the nearest doctor. On the drive home my husband said he thinks we should let him go. I am still afraid, but I also realized that if we say no, he might resent us. He has agreed to a daily video call for the first month and to come home every second weekend. I suppose my question now is how to prepare him and myself for this.

Client wrote on 04/01/2026 at 3:00 PM:
I have two children, a daughter aged 11 and a son aged 8. My daughter is academically gifted — she reads constantly, gets excellent grades, and teachers praise her regularly. My son is more physical and creative but struggles with reading and writing. He was recently tested and shows signs of dyslexia, though the formal assessment is still pending. The problem is that my son has started saying things like you love her more and she gets everything. Last week he destroyed a drawing his sister made for a school project. My daughter then said she wished she was an only child. My husband and I try to be fair, but we spend more time helping our daughter with her advanced projects and homework while our son mostly plays outside. I realize now that our son might interpret this as favouritism. My own parents compared me to my sister constantly, and I swore I would never do that.

Counsellor wrote on 05/01/2026 at 10:45 AM:
Thank you for sharing this. The dynamic you describe is very common in families where children have different strengths and needs. Your son is not wrong in his perception — children are very sensitive to how parental time and attention are distributed, even when parents intend to be fair. The key distinction is between equal treatment and equitable treatment. Equal means giving the same to both; equitable means giving each child what they need. Right now, your daughter's academic achievements receive visible recognition, while your son's strengths in physical activity and creativity may feel invisible to him. I would suggest creating dedicated one-on-one time with each child — even 20 minutes of focused attention daily makes a measurable difference. How does your son express his creativity?

Client wrote on 07/01/2026 at 7:30 PM:
Your question about creativity made me think. My son actually builds the most incredible things with Lego and cardboard — last month he built a working marble run that took him three days. But we never displayed it the way we hang up his sister's art and certificates. I tried dedicated time this week. I spent 30 minutes with my son building something together on Tuesday, and he was so happy he told his friend about it. But my daughter noticed and asked why I was spending so much time with him. She said it is not fair because she has to do her homework alone. I feel like whatever I do, one of them feels hurt.
